title: Suicide rate, by age
subtitle: Estimated annual number of suicides per 100,000 individuals in each age group. Suicide deaths are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data includes modeling based on global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.
caption:
Suicide rate, by age

Estimated annual number of suicides per 100,000 individuals in each age group. Suicide deaths are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data includes modeling based on global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

This chart presents four horizontal bar charts comparing suicide rates by age group for Brazil, South Korea, the United States, and Japan in 2021. The vertical axis lists age brackets ranging from 15-19 to 85+, while the horizontal bars represent the estimated annual number of suicides per 100,000 individuals. In Brazil, the rate peaks at 27.2 for the 85+ group, with the 75-84 group at 15 and younger groups generally below 11. South Korea displays the highest values on the chart, with the 85+ age group reaching 69.4 and the 75-84 group at 63.3; rates decline steadily for younger cohorts, bottoming out at 11.3 for the 15-19 group. The United States shows a different distribution, where the 85+ group leads at 24.5, but the 20-24 and 25-34 age groups also register high rates of 20.1 and 21.2 respectively, creating a cluster of elevated values across the 20 to 54 range. Japan exhibits a distinct pattern where rates remain relatively stable across most adult age groups, hovering between 17.7 and 22.5, before rising to 19 for the 85+ category; the lowest rate for Japan is 11.7 in the 15-19 bracket. Across all four nations, the 15-19 age group consistently records the lowest or near-lowest suicide rates relative to older demographics.
